sections.title: 1. Upright Teardown: Cleaning & Prep
sections.html:
FULL REBUILD LEVEL:

  1. Make sure before pics have been taken by shop photographer.
  2. Designate a labeled piano box (brand and serial number) and disassemble piano. Put all cabinetry together on one shelf with foam protection in between each component. Make sure to label the cabinetry shelf in its correct space on the whiteboard. Every screw goes in a labeled baggie with brand, serial number and where that/those screw/s go. Baggies, cheek blocks and anything else that will fit go in the piano box. Put keys in a labeled piano box. Put action on a shelf. (For your first time removing a spinet action, ask for help.) Leave action screws in a labeled baggie in the box.
  3. Remove lid and check for pin block separation.
  4. Remove all visual metal hardware (except for 5' continuous hinge, if there is one). Put it all in a gallon ziplock, label it with the piano's brand & serial number. This always includes pedals, lock, lock striker plate, and escutcheon (metal key hole). This sometimes also includes some small hinges, pedal trim, knobs. Once you have removed the pedals on step 8, take the labeled bag upstairs to the replating room.
  5. Take “tech” before pics, upload them to the “tech” folder in the google drive folder named for this piano's brand and serial number. Ask the shop manager for instructions on how to upload the pics. Particularly take pics of felt configurations under and through the strings, since those will be removed later.
  6. Vacuum piano. Throw away old punchings, but keep some felt punching samples in box. Remove back rail felt and put in box.
  7. Tip the piano and take off the bottom board.
  8. Clean trap work: (Make sure to keep everything organized and labeled so you can put it back together exactly the same. Take pictures, upload to this piano's google album.) Take the bottom board outside and sodablast. Remove the pedals and put in a bag for replating. Sand / buff each individual piece. Buff all screws and metal. Replace felt / leather and lubricate. Use the planer or vacuum sander to clean the board. Glue/repair cracks if necessary. Replace the bottom panel altogether if cracks are bad enough. Clean pedal rods and replace needed felt. Once finished and put back together, take trapwork upstairs with cabinetry.
  1. Replace the casters with either rubber single- or rubber double-casters. Steinways require rubber double-casters in the back.
  1. Remove keybed. Clean key bed. Take outside and sodablast. Use TSP for stubborn spots and stains. Sand the rest. Use Scotch-Brite for front and balance rail pins to get rid of all corrosion. If the keybed is painted, use TSP and a rag. Replace felts, including back rail cloth.
  2. Remove strings:
  3. Bass strings (ordering new ones from Hellerbass): Fill out a Hellerbass sheet with string measurements. Measure them while they’re still under tension. Only use Hellerbass measuring tape. Put in the “Bass String” file on the wall above the break room desk. See Hellerbass order sheet example.

To remove them, use one of two methods (either way, be careful to avoid scratching the plate or pinblock).

Method 1: use a becket breaker and a hammer.
Method 2: lower the tension of each note to the point that the tuning pin coil gets so loose that it almost starts to wrap around itself. Use needle nose pliers to pry the becket bend up, out of the becket hole. Pull the coil off the tuning pin. Use wire cutters to cut the coil off.

  1. Bass strings (ordering new ones from Schaff): Remove all wound strings (some wound strings may spill over into the tenor section beyond the first break). Remove them IN ORDER, fishing the hitch pin loop of each wound string onto a short length of piano wire with a washer tied to the end as a stop. If there are any missing bass strings, put a cardboard front rail punching on the wire in its place and write “missing string” on the punching.

    To remove them, use one of two methods (either way, be careful to avoid scratching the plate or pinblock).

Method 1: use a becket breaker and a hammer.
Method 2: lower the tension of each note to the point that the tuning pin coil gets so loose that it almost starts to wrap around itself. Use needle nose pliers to pry the becket bend up, out of the becket hole. Pull the coil off the tuning pin. Use wire cutters to cut the coil off.


With all the wound strings on a line, tie the other end of the line into the washer so the strings are secure. Use 4-6 small zip ties every foot or so to tighten all the wound strings together into one fat bundle. Use another 8-10 medium or large zip ties to make a tight wreath out of the bundle. Tie everything down and together so it’s just a circle, without wires poking out. Cut off the tails of the zip ties so it's all neat and easy to ship. Label it with the piano’s name/serial number only on one side of the label. Put it in “BASS STRINGS” bin under the breakroom desk.

  1. Treble strings: Grab a tear-down sheet from the breakroom desk and fill it out. Don’t leave any boxes blank. If there’s none of something, write “none” so we know you didn’t just forget to count. Take pictures of everything, especially hitch pins. See example tear-down sheet on page 63.

Loosen all of the strings so that it's easier to remove the capo bar.

Using one of the 2 string removal methods listed above, being careful not to scratch or mark the plate or pinblock, remove one string at a time, recording the diameter of each string. Mark it down on the tear-down sheet. Note: you only need to measure one string in each trio, since they should all be the same.

  1. Remove tuning pins: Use the large drill with a tuning pin bit to quickly back out all the tuning pins. Wear at least one glove so you can use your hand to stop the drill’s momentum between each tuning pin. Keep 3 samples in a bag. Throw the rest of old tuning pins away.
  1. Remove plate: Find a piece of cardboard (2' x 2' approximately), make a map by drawing the outline of the plate on it and other details that might be important for the understanding of the map. Remove each screw and bolt on the plate and punch them through the cardboard map on their matching location. Action holders also go on the cardboard after they’ve been measured. Write down the action holders measurements on the “map” area of the teardown sheet, indicating from which point of the bolt they were taken to which side of the plate (clock system, see example teardown sheet), measurements should be taken with a caliper. You can also write down these measurements next to each holder on the cardboard, the more detail the better. Label cardboard with piano brand and serial number.

Once all bolts and screws are out, find an available spot to put the plate in. Find the plate cart and 3 to 4 people to help you remove the plate, or use the plate hoist in the main shop. Label the plate using cardstock and string labels found in the labels drawer. Include how many pieces the plate has.

  1. Tip the piano back up, take it outside and blow out with compressed air if necessary.
  2. Use TSP to clean the soundboard and bridges completely.
  3. Sand and polish plate screws and bolts: Sand them on the drill press, start with 80 or 100, do all grits to 2000, then buff on a cotton wheel. Action holders and their knobs only require buffing. *Note: this step may be obsolete by 2025, if so, all metal, including plate screws & bolts and capo bars will be replated as well and left in the replating room alongside visible metal hardware
  4. One last vacuum. Take a picture of finished piano, upload it to the shared google album for this piano.
  5. Clean capo bar. Buff all screws. You can use a combination of techniques to clean the capo bar: first buff, then polish with bright boy. *Never use Scotch-Brite on a capo bar, you’ll never achieve a mirror finish if you do. *Note: this step may be obsolete by 2025, if so, all metal, including plate screws & bolts and capo bars will be replated as well and left in the replating room alongside visible metal hardware

sections.title: 1a. Upright Action Prep
sections.html:
FULL REBUILD LEVEL:

  1. Put action in a cradle.
  2. Cut off dampers (Brigham prefers steam, as long as the action is really well protected. Everything must be masked off so only the dampers are exposed):
  3. Razor blade method: Flip action upside down. Wear a leather glove on your left hand. Hold damper head in left hand and cut with fresh blade.You can usually tear off the bulk of the bass dampers. Then cut the rest.
  4. Steam method: Flip action upside down. Find the steamer, plug it in and turn on. While the steamer heats up, prep the action by covering everything but the dampers with foam sheets. Steam off the damper felts with the help of tweezers.
  5. Remove hammer rest rail, then sodablast action.
  6. Remove spring rail and let off button rail.

*Make needed repairs at an appropriate time.

  1. Unhook bridle straps with pliers.
  2. Using a thin sharpie, number only the “section ends” hammers at the wooden tail. (e.g. 1 and 26, 27 and 53, 54 and 88). Remove them, tape each section end pair together and store in the piano box.
  3. Remove all hammer butts and hammers. We keep these hammers as salvage. Put them in a little box and label it to match the other little boxes on the salvage shelf.
  4. Tighten all screws while the hammer butts are still out (action should be in a cradle to ensure that you can access all screws).
  5. Install new hammer butts. Check the pinning on each one with an old hammer and shank.
  6. Travel the hammer butts: Align hammer butts to be parallel and equidistant from each other. Take your time on this. This is an important step. To ensure they are aligned and equidistant, loosen the hammer flange screw, move the butt to the desired position, then retighten. To ensure the catchers are perfectly parallel, use travel paper under the hammer flange.
  7. Using duckbill pliers, check each jack flange firmly, use a decent amount of force. If you hear any cracking, that is the glue joint, and all jacks need to be reglued. Only remove top screw of whippen to glue. Use duckbill or parallel pliers at the whippen/jack glue joint to remove jacks. Use wood glue. Ensure jacks are firmly into their whippen saddle. IMPORTANT: the back of the jack should line up in the middle of the backcheck. If not, it won't contact the center of the hammer butt; when looking at the jack flange sideways it should be sitting in a 90° angle in relation to the whippen.
  8. Replace any needed felts on whippens. Check that the pins aren’t sticking out of the sides of the whippen flanges.
  9. Replace ALL let off buttons and let off eye-screws. Use a drill, and the pliers from the rebuilding drawer.
  10. Install let-off rail. Slide the let off rail back or forward so the jack toes are contacting the center of all let off buttons. On all connection points of the little arms that come out of the action to hold the let off rail, tighten the screws very, very snug.
  11. Lubricate action: remove damper lever rod, clean it (you can use a combination of Scotch-Brite, buffer or bright boy). Be careful not to bend the hinges. Use CLP to lubricate the bushings where this rod hooks on to. Use teflon powder to lubricate the damper lever felts and spoons. Put rod back in. Use CLP to soak the felt where the damper spring sits in front of the damper.
  12. Remove the old cloth from the rest rail
  13. Clean/sand/polish (depending on the material and condition) the visual surface of the hammer rest rail.
  14. Install new rest rail cloth. Burn frizzies.
  15. Attach bridle straps. Use fingers or large tweezers to move the jack into correct position as you attach the strap.
  16. Replace spring rail felt.
  17. Put action back in storage. Label the rest rail and spring rail and keep with the action. Do not install rest rail and spring rail.

sections.title: 1b. Upright Teardown: Cleaning & Prep
sections.html:
HYBRID LEVEL:

  1. Make sure before pics have been taken by shop photographer.
  2. Designate a labeled piano box (brand and serial number) and disassemble piano. Put all cabinetry together on one shelf with foam protection in between each component. Make sure to label the cabinetry shelf in its correct space on the whiteboard. Every screw goes in a labeled baggie with brand, serial number and where that/those screw/s go. Baggies, cheek blocks and anything else that will fit go in the piano box. Put keys in a labeled piano box. Put action on a shelf. (For your first time removing a spinet action, ask for help.) Leave action screws in a labeled baggie in the box.
  3. Remove lid and check for pin block separation.
  4. Remove all visual metal hardware (except for 5' continuous hinge, if there is one). Put it all in a gallon ziplock, label it with the piano's brand & serial number. This always includes pedals, lock, lock striker plate, and escutcheon (metal key hole). This sometimes also includes some small hinges, pedal trim, knobs. Once you have removed the pedals on step 12, take the labeled bag upstairs to the replating room.
  5. Take “tech” before pics, upload them to the “tech” folder in the google drive folder named for this piano's brand and serial number. Ask the shop manager for instructions on how to upload the pics. Particularly take pics of felt configurations under and through the strings, since those will be removed later.
  6. Vacuum piano. Throw away old punchings, but keep some felt punching samples in box. Remove back rail felt and put in box.
  7. Clean key bed. Remove only the frame, take outside and sodablast. Use TSP for stubborn spots and stains on frame and keybed. Sand the rest. Use Scotch- Brite for front and balance rail pins, get rid of all corrosion. If the keybed is painted, use TSP and a rag. Screw frame back into the keybed. Replace felts, including back rail cloth.
  8. Shine strings. Use coarse Scotch-Brite, then also use steel wool on the bass strings to give an extra shine.
  9. Shine tuning pins with Scotch-Brite.
  10. Clean the plate with TSP.
  11. Tip the piano and take off the bottom board.
  12. Clean trap work: (Make sure to keep everything organized and labeled so you can put it back together exactly the same. Take pictures, upload to this piano's google album) Take the bottom board outside and sodablast. Remove the pedals and put in a bag for replating, if they only need polishing just buff. Sand / buff each individual piece. Buff all screws and metal. Replace felt / leather and lubricate.Use the planer or vacuum sander to clean the board. Glue/repair cracks if necessary. Replace this the bottom panel altogether if cracks are bad enough. Clean pedal rods and replace needed felt.
  1. Replace the casters with either rubber single- or rubber double-casters. Steinways require rubber double-casters in the back.
  1. Put trap work back on piano and tip up right.
  2. Clean the back of piano with TSP. Use a magnet to check for any little pieces of metal trash that might cause buzzing.
  3. All other hardware inside the piano, such as action posts, action nose bolts, wooden trapwork rods, wooden trapwork rod holders, action supports, and possibly more: use sandpaper, Scotch-Brite, or buffing to make it all look new
  4. Take the piano outside and blow out with compressed air, if possible.
  5. On the drill press, sand then buff all plate screws and bolts that you're able to reach (since the strings are still on in this column, you may be unable to reach many of them). Start with 80 or 100, do all grits to 2000, then buff on a cotton wheel. Remove, polish, and replace them one by one.
  6. For the capo bar (the rail beneath the tuning pins), shine with bright boy. If needed, remove, buff and replace each screw one at a time.
  7. Action holders and their knobs only require buffing.
  8. One last vacuum. Take a picture of the finished piano, upload it to the shared google album for this piano.

sections.title: 1c. Upright Action Prep
sections.html:
HYBRID LEVEL:

  1. Put action in a cradle.
  2. Cut off dampers (Brigham prefers steam, as long as the action is really well protected. Everything must be masked off so only the dampers are exposed):
  3. Razor blade method: Flip action upside down. Wear leather glove on left hand. Hold damper head in left hand and cut with fresh blade in blade holder. You can usually tear off the bulk of the bass dampers. Then cut the rest.
  4. Steam method: Flip action upside down. Find the steamer, plug it in and turn on. While the steamer heats up, prep the action by covering everything but the dampers with foam sheets. Steam off the damper felts with the help of tweezers

.

  1. Remove hammer rest rail, then sodablast action.
  2. Remove spring rail and let off button rail.

*Make needed repairs at an appropriate time.

  1. Unhook bridle straps with pliers.
  2. Using a thin sharpie, number only the “section ends” hammers at the wooden tail. (e.g. 1 and 26, 27 and 53, 54 and 88). Remove them, tape each section end pair together and leave aside.
  3. Number all hammer butts (including section ends) with a pencil.
  4. Remove all hammers. Stack the hammers in front of you on the table.
  5. Bolster all hammer butts to remove indentations in the leather from the jacks.
  6. Unless the catcher leather is in decent condition, remove all catcher leather with a blade. (Note: even if only the leather in the middle section is bad you still need to replace all of them.)
  7. Use pvc-e glue to glue new leather to the catchers. Make sure they are cut at right angles, they are beautiful and consistent, and that no glue got onto the outward-facing surface of the leather. If it does, that piece will have to be replaced with a different piece. You can use one of the small guillotines to cut the pieces consistently and faster.
  8. Wiggle all catchers, carefully feeling for a glue joint that needs to be reglued. If you feel even the slightest give, work it loose and reglue it with wood glue. Be sure the glue joint is exactly where it was by comparing it with another hammer butt, ensuring the dimensions are exactly the same. If you don't, the reglued catcher will often stick out further than its neighbors.
  9. Consider replacing all the hammer flanges. If they are really worn, old, or cracking, then replace them. If they are in good condition, we'll use the originals. If it’s somewhere in the middle, consult shop manager.
  10. If we're going to be using the original hammer flanges, repin them. Keep section ends separate. Make sure section ends are also numbered on the hammerhead.
  11. Cut shanks, except for section ends. Keep heads on section ends and put them in piano box.
  12. Remove bridle straps.
  13. Bore hammer butts. Use the jig and drill press.
  14. Glue bridle straps into the hammer butts.
  15. Using duckbill pliers, check some of the jack flanges firmly, use a decent amount of force. If you hear any cracking, that is the glue joint, and all jacks need to be reglued. Only remove top screw of whippen to glue. Use duckbill or parallel pliers at the whippen/jack glue joint to remove jacks. Use wood glue. Ensure jacks are firmly into their whippen saddle. IMPORTANT: the back of the jack should line up in the middle of the backcheck. If not, it won't contact the center of the hammer butt; when looking at the jack flange sideways it should be sitting in a 90° angle in relation to the whippen.
  16. Replace any needed felts on whippens. Check that the pins aren’t sticking out of the sides of the whippen flanges.
  17. Tighten all screws while the hammer butts are still out (action should be in a cradle to ensure that you can access all screws).
  18. Lay out hammer butts on table in groups of 0 to 9, 10 to 19, etc. Install hammer butts. Ensure they are all parallel and equidistant from each other. Loosen the hammer flange screw, move the butt to the desired position, then retighten. To ensure the catchers are perfectly parallel, use travel paper under the hammer flange.
  19. Let off rail:
    If let off buttons are in good condition (not cracking, seem strong), but let off felt is in poor condition: replace just the let off felt with new let off felt that is the same thickness and hopefully (but not necessarily) the same diameter. Use plenty of glue! Be aware that let off felt tends to absorb a lot of the glue, leaving too little for a good, permanent bond.

    If let off buttons are in poor condition: replace buttons with brand new buttons that already have new felt pre-installed.
  20. Lubricate action: remove damper lever rod, clean it (you can use a combination of Scotch-Brite, buffer or bright boy). Be careful not to bend the hinges. Use CLP to lubricate the bushings where this rod hooks on to. Use teflon powder to lubricate the damper lever felts and spoons. Put rod back in. Use CLP to soak the felt where the damper spring sits in front of the damper.
  21. Install let-off rail. Slide the let off rail back or forward so the jack toes are contacting the center of all let off buttons. On all connection points of the little arms that come out of the action to hold the let off rail, tighten the screws very, very snug.
  22. Remove the old cloth from the rest rail.
  23. Clean/sand/polish (depending on the material and condition) the visual surface of the hammer rest rail.
  24. Install new rest rail cloth. Burn frizzies.
  25. Attach bridle straps. Use fingers or large tweezers to move the jack into correct position as you attach the strap.
  26. Replace spring rail felt if needed.
  27. Put action back in piano. Label the rest rail and spring rail and keep with the piano or in storage with piano cabinetry. Do not install rest rail and spring rail.

  6. Write down the following information about the damper felts in the treble section: which dampers have trichord felt (specifiy if it's in the back, front or both), do this by indicating the damper number (left to right), A0 being damper one. Remove dampers in order and leave on damper holder or on cardboard.


  7. Remove Strings
  8. Bass strings (ordering new ones from Hellerbass): Fill out a Hellerbass sheet with string measurements. Measure them while they’re still under tension. Put in the “Bass String” file on the wall above the break room desk.

Method 1: use a becket breaker and a hammer.

With all the wound strings on a line, tie the other end of the line into the washer so the strings are secure. Use 4-6 small zip ties every foot or so to tighten all the wound strings together into one fat bundle. Use another 8-10 medium or large zip ties to make a tight wreath out of the bundle. Tie everything down and together so it’s just a circle, without wires poking out. Cut off the tails of the zip ties so its all neat and easy to ship. Label it with the piano’s name/serial number only on one side of the label. Put it in “BASS STRINGS” bin under the breakroom desk.

  1. Treble strings: Grab a tear-down sheet from the breakroom desk and fill it out (see example at end of book). Don’t leave any boxes blank. If there’s none of something, write “none” so we know you didn’t just forget to count.

Using one of the 2 string removal methods listed above, being careful not to scratch or mark the plate or pinblock, remove one string at a time, recording the diameter of each string. Mark it down on the tear-down sheet. Note: you only need to measure one string in each trio, since they should all be the same.

  1. Remove tuning pins: Use the large drill with a tuning pin bit to quickly back out all the tuning pins. Wear at least one glove so you can use your hand to stop the drill’s momentum between each tuning pin. Keep 3 samples in a bag.Throw the rest of old tuning pins away.
  1. Remove Agraffes: With the help of an agraffe tool, remove all agraffes and put them in a baggie, label the baggie with brand and serial number and with the quantity of each kind of agraffe: monochord, bichord and trichord. Have them thrown in the tumbler for polishing and label the tumbler bin with the same info. Do not leave in tumbler more than 8 hrs. Tumblr will be turned off at the end of the day.
  2. Remove plate: Find a piece of cardboard (2' x 2' approximately), make a map by drawing the outline of the plate on it and other details that might be important for the understanding of the map. Remove each screw and bolt on the plate and punch them through the cardboard map on their matching location. Label cardboard with piano brand and serial number.

Once all bolts and screws are out, find an available spot to put the plate in. Find the plate cart and 3 to 4 people to help you remove the plate, or use the plate hoist in the main shop. (If it's a concert grand you need to use the plate hoist.) Label the plate using cardstock and string labels found in the pencils and labels drawer.

  1. Take the piano outside and blow out with compressed air, if possible.
  2. If there’s a soundboard decal let the manager know and/or take 2 pictures of it with a ruler indicating vertical and horizontal measurements so that a new one can be ordered.
  3. Use TSP to clean the soundboard and bridges completely.

Polish pedal rods. Put lyre in storage. *Make sure to keep everything organized and labeled so you can put it back together exactly the same. Take pictures, upload to this piano's google album “tech folder”.
  5. Buff casters or replace if necessary. *casters can be removed easily by one person. Remove the lyre, place a sturdy bench next to a leg underneath the piano. Use the car jack (with wood/felt scraps to protect the bench and the underside of the piano) to raise the piano up. Remove leg from piano, remove caster from leg, reinstall leg. Repeat this process for the other 2 legs.
  1. Clean key bed and key frame. Throw away old felt punchings on key frame but keep some felt punching samples in box. Remove back rail felt and put in box. Soda blast. Use TSP and Scotch-Brite for balance rail and front rail. Sand the rest. If the key bed/frame is painted, use TSP and a rag.
  2. Shine tips of pins on front rail and balance rail with Scotch-Brite. Straighten the front rail pins.
  3. Replace felts, including back rail felt.

  1. One last vacuum. Take a picture of the finished piano, upload it to the shared google album for this piano.

sections.title: 2a. Grand Action Prep
sections.html:
FULL REBUILD LEVEL:

  1. Cut off damper felts. Make sure you've already written down damper info explained on step 4 in Grand Cleaning & Prep.
  2. Wear leather glove on left hand. Hold damper head in left hand and cut with fresh blade in blade holder.
  3. Sodablast action stack.

*Make needed repairs at an appropriate time.

  1. Remove rest rail and let off button rail.
  2. Number and remove hammers. Keep section ends separate.
  3. Remove whippens.
  4. Tighten all screws while the hammers are still out.
  5. Remove the old cloth from the rest rail. Install new rest rail cloth and burn frizzies.
  6. Once new hammers and flanges and whippens arrive, install.
  7. If whippen heels come separately, glue those on first using an old sample as a guide for location.
  8. Install whippens.
  9. Install hammer shanks and flanges.
  10. Replace let off buttons and let off eye-screws.
  11. Put let off rail back in the action.
  12. Lubricate action.
  13. Put action back on shelf.
