Make sure to label cabinetry shelf on it's correct space on the whiteboard. Every screw goes in a labeled baggie with brand, serial number and where that/those screw/s go. Baggies, cheekblocks and anything else that will fit go in the piano box. Put keys in a labeled piano box. Put action stack on a table while cleaning. Leave action screws in a labeled baggie in the box.
  3. Remove all visual metal hardware (except for 5' continuous hinge, if there is one). Put it all in a gallon ziplock, label it with the piano's brand & serial number, leave in replating room. This always includes pedals, lock, lock striker plate, and escutcheon (metal key hole). This sometimes also includes some small hinges, pedal trim, knobs.
  4. Take “tech” before pics, upload them to the “tech” folder in the google drive folder named for this piano's brand and serial number. Ask the shop manager for instructions on how to upload the pics. Particularly take pics of felt configurations under and through the strings, since those will be removed later.
  5. Write down the following information about the damper felts in the treble section: which dampers have trichord felt (specify if it's in the back, front or both), do this by indicating the damper number (left to right), A0 being damper one. Remove dampers in order and leave on damper holder or on cardboard.
  6. Shine strings. Use coarse Scotch-Brite, then also use steel wool on the bass strings to give an extra shine.
  7. Use an old paint brush with the vacuum: stir up as much dust and debris with one hand and vacuum it up with the other. Do this to as much of the plate, soundboard, strings, tuning pins, and action cavity as you can reach.
  8. Vacuum under the soundboard: tops of the large support beams, above the rear leg, trapwork, etc.
  9. Soundboard Cleaning: How you do the next step will depend on what type of finish the soundboard and plate have and what condition they are in:
  10. Option 1: older pianos with dust really caked on the soundboard, clear coat on the soundboard is gone or in poor condition, sometimes with spills or stains, poor condition plate and soundboard:

    Use masking tape to mask off all gold-colored, painted plate surfaces, with the exception of the tuning pin area.

Mask off black (graphite) surfaces in the center of bridges.

Put the piano up on its side and take it outside.

Soda blast the soundboard and bridges-be thorough enough that you don't leave any streaks or stripes.

From a distance (so you don't remove gold paint), soda blast around the tuning pins and other tight, hard-to-clean areas, at your discretion.* You need to find the happy-medium where the dust and debris is removed but the paint remains intact. The distance and pressure you use will vary from piano to piano.

  1. Option 2: newer/somewhat newer pianos with dust that once removed with a comparatively easy swipe, reveals a nicely lacquered soundboard and plate:

Use the 3 soundboard squeegees with red felt (1 large, 1 medium, 1 small) to squeegee the soundboard:

Wearing ear protection, leave the vacuum running the whole time. Insert the squeegee, wipe the soundboard, pull it out and vacuum off the dust. Repeat, repeat, repeat.

Fold lots of pieces of paper towel to make lots of fairly small squares, then use these with a sharpened hammer shank and some cleaner to detail the soundboard. Push the little paper towel squares around on the soundboard with the hammer shank (and pull them out with tweezers when each square is saturated) until you've covered all of the soundboard. Be careful to avoid getting any cleaner on the coils of the bass strings.

  1. Clean the plate with TSP. Be conservative around the tuning pins to avoid allowing any cleaner to seep to the bass of the tuning pins and into the pinblock underneath.
  2. Use Q-tips and a little plastic cup of cleaner to detail the plate and bridges in hard-to-reach areas. Go through 20-50 (or more!) Q-tips.

*The goal is to minimize this, as it is time-consuming. As long as you're not removing gold paint with the soda blaster, go for it in these hard-to-reach areas!

**Use very little cleaner around the tuning pins and the strings. If cleaner gets around the tuning pins, it can leak down the pin into the pinblock, and the pins would then have to be replaced.

  1. Rebuild trapwork. Remove and clean the lyre. Buff all screws and metal. Replace felt / leather and lubricate. Do the same with the other half of the trapwork still attached to the piano. Polish pedal rods. Put lyre in storage. *Make sure to keep everything organized and labeled so you can put it back together exactly the same. Take pictures, upload to this piano's google album.
  1. Buff casters. *casters can be removed easily by one person. Remove the lyre, place a sturdy bench next to a leg underneath the piano. Use the car jack (with wood/felt scraps to protect the bench and the underside of the piano) to raise the piano up. Remove leg from piano, remove caster from leg, reinstall leg. Repeat this process for the other 2 legs.
  1. Clean key bed and key frame. Throw away old felt punchings on key frame but keep some felt punching samples in box. Remove back rail felt and put in box. Soda blast. Use TSP and Scotch-Brite for balance rail and front rail. Sand the rest. If the key bed/frame is painted, use TSP and a rag.
  2. Shine tips of pins on front rail and balance rail with Scotch-Brite. Straighten the front rail pins.
  3. Replace felts, including back rail felt.
  4. On the drill press, sand then buff all plate screws and bolts that you're able to reach (since the strings are still on, you may be unable to reach many of them). Remove, polish, and replace them one by one. Start with 80 or 100, do all grits to 2000, then buff on a cotton wheel.
  5. One last vacuum. Take a picture of the finished piano, upload it to the shared google album for this piano.

sections.title: 2c. Grand Action Prep
sections.html:
HYBRID LEVEL:

  1. Cut off damper felts. Make sure you've already written down damper info explained on step 4 in Grand Cleaning & Prep.
  2. Wear leather glove on left hand. Hold damper head in left hand and cut with fresh blade in blade holder.
  3. Sodablast action stack.

*Make needed repairs at an appropriate time.

  1. Remove rest rail and let off button rail.
  2. Number and remove hammers. Keep section ends separate.
  3. Give a sample hammer and flange to shop manager for ordering new parts.
  4. Tighten all screws while the hammers are still out.
  5. Remove the old cloth from the rest rail. Install new rest rail cloth and burn frizzies.
  6. Once new hammers and flanges arrive, install them.
  7. Let off rail:


If let off buttons are in good condition (not cracking, seem strong), but let off felt is in poor condition: replace just the let off felt with new let off felt that is the same thickness and hopefully (but not necessarily) the same diameter. Use plenty of glue! Be aware that let off felt tends to absorb a lot of the glue, leaving too little for a good, permanent bond.


If let off buttons are in poor condition: replace buttons with brand new buttons that already have new felt pre-installed.

  1. Put let off rail back in the action
  2. Lubricate action. Brush teflon powder on all knuckles.
  3. Put action back in piano.

sections.title: 3. Upright and Grand Refurbish
sections.html:
*Much personal discretion is required for refurbishing. Take an overall look at the piano and see what needs cleaning, what needs replacing, and what needs polishing. Only replace parts if they are broken.

Cleaning and Prep

  1. Take before pics. Take pics throughout.
  2. Remove and store cabinetry
  3. Remove lid and check for pin block separation (upright)
  4. Remove action and leave on table in a cradle
  5. Clean as much as you can without removing screws, be careful around the tuning pins not to get any oil based cleaners into the pinblock, don’t get any liquid cleaning products on the bass strings.
  6. Clean Strings with Scotch Brite
  7. Clean Capo Bar or agraffes with Brite Boy
  8. Clean and polish pedals with Brite Boy
  9. Vacuum Keybed
  10. Temporarily remove keys
  11. Check if bushings need to be replaced
  12. Vacuum trapwork
  13. Lubricate trapwork
  14. Clean keys with windex or TSP
  15. If not being refinished, clean the back with TSP (upright)



Action Prep

  1. Fix any glaring action problems
  2. Square and space hammers roughly
  3. Tighten all action screws
  4. Reshape Hammers
  1. Blow out action outside
  2. Lubricate action
  3. Clean/sand/polish (depending on the material and condition) the visual surface of the hammer rest rail
  4. Bolster hammer butts/knuckles if needed
  5. Check pinning
  6. Travel hammers
  7. Put action back in piano
  8. Align hammers to strings. Finalize squaring and spacing.
  9. Regulate (see pages 35 and 45 for upright or grand regulation)

sections.title: 4. Upright Damper Installation
sections.html:
1. Align and tighten damper heads.
  2. Using bending pliers, ensure that each damper head is straight, aligned with its neighbor, equal spaces in between each damper (make a compromise between ensuring that each damper is poised directly over the center of its strings and ensuring that the spaces between each damper are exactly the same.)
  3. Ensure that each damper is parallel with its string.
  4. Tighten all damper set screws.
  1. Install bass dampers.
  2. Use triple dampers, 2-1/4" long. These are installed with the action in the piano.
  3. On the new bass damper out of the box, you have to cut the green felt in the back that will accept the glue to decrease thickness.
  4. Work some Quick and Thick glue into the felt of the damper, place it on the string & slide it down the string, under its damper head. The damper spring will act as its clamp while the glue dries.
  5. First break dampers.
  6. For the 6 or 7 dampers after the 1st break, install trichord wedges. Look at other pianos we've done for an example.
  7. Use the white damper miter box to cut the damper strips to the correct size and shape.
  8. Install these like you installed the bass dampers, working glue into the backing, putting the string side onto the string, then sliding the damper down the string into position. The damper head will act as the clamp.
  9. While these dampers are more efficient at dampening, they can be touchy. Accordingly, you may have to do some ironing and clipping of the felt. This is a skill to be mastered in itself. Talk with the training manager.
  1. Flat dampers.
  2. With PVC-E glue, install flat dampers outside of the piano, with the action in a cradle and face up (so gravity holds the dampers on while the glue is drying).
  3. Install the flat dampers on the rest of the damper heads (Be aware that they're in order in the box they come in-NOT in random order.) Begin by finding the damper that fits the last and smallest damper head and start gluing from there up.
  4. Choose dampers that align best size-wise with their corresponding damper heads. Don't necessarily just start with the largest damper for the largest head or the smallest damper for the smallest head.
  5. Regulate damper timing while hammers, rest rail and spring rail are still off the action. That way you will only need to touch up this step during regulation. Put the action back in the piano.
  6. Bend damper wires only at very top and very bottom.
  7. Watching dampers, slowly engage dampers with the pedal or by hand by gently pulling up on connection rod.
  8. Look for early dampers.
  9. Fix early dampers by gently bending damper wire forward at the bottom of the wire.
  10. Look for late dampers.
  11. Fix late dampers by gently bending damper wire back at the bottom of the wire.
  12. Keep fixing the worst, then the next worst, etc., until dampers engage at exactly the same time, even when you engage the damper pedal very, very gently.
  13. Make the damper heads parallel to the strings by bending at the top of the wire.
  1. Take action out of the piano. Install spring rail (in the correct direction) and rest rail. Give the springs on the spring rail a slight bend to make them stronger before installing. Put action back in piano.

sections.title: 5. Upright Hammer Prep
sections.html:
Note: if this is your first time ask the training manager or shop manager for assistance.

  1. Unwrap hammers, make sure they are upright hammers (hammers can be mislabeled). You can tell by the length of the bass hammers. Upright bass hammers are shorter than the treble, whereas grand bass hammers are longer than the treble.
  2. Using the old section ends as reference, number with a pencil and separate the hammers by section. Write the number on the bottom side (side with printed number). Number them even if the number you write down is the same as the printed one. Your handwritten number is going to be the one that counts.
  3. Create a worksheet of the string angle in each section, number of hammers in each section, previous hammer angles, and previous bore distance (distance from the felt tip of the hammer to the center of the shank hole). To determine this piano's original bore distance, use your best judgment to estimate how much to add to your bore distance measurement to account for worn/lost felt and/or reshaping that may have been done to this piano. *Bass and treble have different bore distances.
  4. Measure bass section string angle, divide that in 2 (the result usually varies between 15°-18°), add that to your worksheet.





  5. In the middle section, where the strings fan, measure the first, steepest string. Add that to your worksheet. Then gradually reach 0° by reducing the degree every two hammers. For example:

    Let’s say note n° 29 is the first in the middle section (steepest) and it measures at a 9° angle. On your worksheet you add the following:

    29 - 9° 37 - 5° 45 - 1°
    30 - 9° 38 - 5° 46 - 1°
    31 - 8° 39 - 4° 47 - 0°
    32 - 8° 40 - 4°
    33 - 7° 41 - 3°
    34 - 7° 42 - 3°
    35 - 6° 43 - 2°
    36 - 6° 44 - 2°
  6. Now that you have all the measurements on your worksheet, mark with a pencil the bore distance only on the section ends. Align an entire section in front of you, you can use two straight edges and small clamps to keep them straight and still. Using another straight edge draw a line across all the hammers between the section ends. Now you have bore distance on all the hammers in this section.

Optional: you can also mark the exact center of the bore distance line with a dot or hole made with an awl to use as a guide on the drill press.

  1. Pre-voice the hammers. Use the prevoicing jig. Clamp an entire section of hammers on the jig. Quick pass with a 320 grit sanding paper strip. Then with a voicing tool, stab the hammers about 5 times on each side.
  1. Bore the hammers on the drill press. Take your worksheet with you. Make sure to have some dummy hammers to try out before the official hammers. Find a sharp, brad point (important) drill bit for the desired fit (snug is 0.218", looser is 0.228"). On the drill press and jig you will set position, depth, and rake angle.
  2. Taper bass hammers on the table saw using the tapering jig.

sections.title: 6. Upright Hammer Hanging
sections.html:
Note: if this is your first time ask the training manager or shop manager for assistance.

  1. Select shanks, make sure they’re not extremely crooked by rolling them on a flat surface, but don’t be too picky either.
  2. Set the hammer hanging jigs using the old section ends.
  3. Use the knurler to better fit the shanks into the hammers if necessary. You don’t want them too snug or too loose.
  4. Glue shanks to hammers. Work by section. Use Quick and Thick. Tip: make sure the jig is determining the position of the hammer, not the glue. There should be no pressure on the hammer/shank. After applying the glue and twisting to achieve a collar, lightly set or even drop (LIGHTLY) the hammer into the jig. Only make delicate adjustments.
  5. Once they are dry, determine the shank length by dry fitting with some masking tape hammer 88 and cutting/sanding down the shank until you achieve the best sounding strike point on the string (this is usually just under the lip on the plate).
  6. With a small ruler measure from the rest rail to the top of the hammer tail. Cut its section end to the same height. For the middle section do the same, on the angled section end measure from the rest rail to the highest corner of the hammer tail as reference. Sometimes, the tenor side of the middle section needs to swoop up a little to avoid the dampers.
  7. Dry fit the bass section ends by determining at what height they bisect the strings. Make sure the shank is straight up and down.
  8. Put section ends back into the hanging jig alongside their section, clamp it, using a straight edge draw a line on all the hammers between the section ends, take it to the bandsaw and cut the rest of the shanks. Tip: it's better if all the shanks in between the section ends are slightly shorter.
  9. Once you have all your shanks cut to length use the knurler to better fit the shanks into the hammerbutts if necessary. Having them on the looser end works better.
  10. Clamp all the catchers in a section. Dry fit the hammers of the section. Use an extra “rest rail” piece from the hammer hanging box to make the hammers come closer to the strings, use rubber mutes under the rest rail holders to push the hammers against the strings. This will work as a clamp for hanging hammers.
  1. Glue the section ends. Then glue the middle hammer, use a straight edge to make sure they’re at the same height. Continue gluing the hammers by middles, do not go in a consecutive line. Three things to check while hanging hammers: height with straight edge, shank should be in a straight 90° from the rest rail when looked at from the front, and straight angle hammer from the rest rail when looked at from the top. Don’t worry too much about hammer alignment to string, we will deal with that later.
  2. Repeat on all sections.
  3. Once you have hung all of your hammers, align hammers to strings, square and space hammers. Remember to start with the worst one, then the next worse. Never in a consecutive line.

sections.title: 7. Upright Regulation
sections.html:
1. Square, space and level keys. Tools required: key straight edge, little key height jigs, punching lifter, box of punchings, small phillips screwdriver (for adjustment of little plastic jigs), large flat head screwdriver (for squaring keys), small hammer (squaring), key spacer that fits in multi tool.
  1. Install keyslip.
  2. Only key #1 and #88: Adjust height of both of these keys to be identical. Height must be between 23/32” and 27/32” above the top of the keyslip (if you’re using a caliper it’s between .718” and .841”). Within these guidelines, favor what the piano has currently.
  3. Temporarily remove all paper and felt front rail punchings under these 2 notes.
  4. Place little plastic jigs with a small screw under these notes.
  5. Adjust these jigs’ screws so the screws are precisely touching the underside of the keys. Not pushing up on the keys, not allowing the keys to be depressed even slightly. Exactly touching.
  6. Place straight edge between these keys.
  7. Level all white keys according to this straight edge.
  8. Square and space these keys as you go.
  9. Place ½” sharp segment jigs on keys #1 and #88 OR place little jigs under sharp ends and adjust their height so that the wooden part (where the number is stamped) is level and flush with its neighboring white keys.
  10. Place straight edge on these sharp segment jigs OR directly on sharp ends if you’re using the second method.
  11. Level sharps according to this straight edge.
  12. Touch-up worst square/space/level issues.
  13. Choosing rest rail height. Tools required: 10mm dip block (.394"), aftertouch gauge: either the multi-colored strips or punchings of 0.025" and 0.050", 3 different thicknesses of cloth, pvc-e glue or quick and thick, felt chopper/cutter/scissors, let off regulator, let off magnet, capstan regulator.
  1. Set dip on 3 samples only: C2, C4 & C6
  2. On samples: temporarily remove front rail felt, making dip unnaturally deep, and temporarily pull back the backchecks so they are far out of the way.
  3. Set let off on samples to 1/8" with magnet on strings as gauge.
  4. Replace front rail felt, ensure dip is correctly set on samples with dip block.
  5. Check for aftertouch of approx. .025"-.050".
  6. If aftertouch is non-existent or insufficient, choose at least 3 thicknesses of cloth.
  7. Temporarily place the thinnest cloth under one of the 3 or 4 hammer rest rail supports.
  8. Regulate capstans on the 3 samples according to new rest rail position.
  9. Again, check for aftertouch of approx. .025"-.050".
  10. If aftertouch is still non-existent or insufficient, try the next thicker cloth.
  11. Regulate capstans on the 3 samples according to new rest rail position.
  12. Repeat with thicker and thicker cloth until aftertouch is correct.
  13. Note: with a .025" cardboard punching placed on top of front rail felt of each sample, the jack on these notes will still barely pull out from under the hammer butt when key is slowly, fully depressed. With a .050" cardboard punching placed under each sample, the jack will not escape at all.
  14. Regulate all lost motion by adjusting capstans. Tools required: capstan regulator.
  1. Jack barely touches the bottom of the hammer butt.
  2. Jack must not put pressure on the hammer butt.
  3. Hammer shank must be resting on the hammer rest rail, not the jack.
  4. As soon as a key is depressed, even slightly, the jack will immediately put pressure on the hammer butt.
  5. Test for capstans that are too high by gently tugging back on the hammer rest rail. If capstans are too high, hammer butts will be resting on the jack instead of the hammer rest rail, and thus will not follow the tugging on the rest rail.
  6. Regulate let off. Tools required: flat head screwdriver to tighten let off rail screws, let off regulator, let off magnet, 1"x1"x12" wooden stick to hold hammers closer to strings, rubber or felt mute to hold rest rail at exact position about 3/16" from strings.
  1. Ensure all let off rail screws are very tight, and that center of jack toes are contacting the center of the let off buttons.
  2. Place 1/8” thick magnetic strip against strings at the point at which the hammers strike the strings.
  3. Place 1” x 1” x 12” stick in between hammer rest rail and shanks.
  4. Place a rubber mute under hammer rest rail supports such that hammers are resting 1/16”-1/8” from the magnet strip.
  5. Adjust let off buttons.
  6. Play key very slowly.
  7. Adjust its let off screw such that the hammer barely kisses the magnetic strip before it pulls back.
  8. Ensure hammers do not bear on the magnetic strip at all before they let off.
  9. Align backchecks. Tools required: Wire bending pliers (smiling pliers).
  1. Only bend backcheck wires at very top and very bottom of wire.
  2. Bend wire so backcheck and catcher engage each other in the dead center.
  3. Left/right alignment and squaring are completed at the same time.
  4. Set a few samples in each section that on a mezzo-forte blow, the backcheck will comfortably hold the catcher at ½ - ¾ blow distance.
  5. Move all other backchecks in each section to the approximate position of samples.
  6. Put your head down at the level of the backchecks, sighting down the line of backchecks.
  7. Fix the worst, then the next worst, then the next worst, until you reach a straight line of backchecks-like soldiers.
  8. Regulate Dip. Tools required: aftertouch gauge (either multi-colored strips or 2 punchings of .025" & .050" thickness), punching lifter, box of punchings.
  9. Regulate key dip on all whites and sharps according to aftertouch.
  10. Whites first.
  11. Use the .030”/.050” gauge or .025” and .050” cardboard punchings to regulate dip on each individual key such that at .025”, the jack still barely slips out from under the hammer butt, whereas at .050”, when the key bottoms out on the gauge, the jack doesn’t come out.
  12. Remove all white keys, leaving only the sharps.
  13. Repeat dip regulation on sharps.
  14. Reinstall whites.
  15. Damper lift regulation. Note: This procedure is often less-necessary on pianos that are less than ~60 years old. On pianos that are newer than 60 years, at least check it to see if improvements can be made. Often, damper lift on these pianos will already be pretty good. Tools required: 2 multi-tool handles, right angle lego hand tool (straight shank), right angle lego hand tool (30-60 degree shank), spring tool.
  1. Bend damper wires only at the very top and very bottom.
  2. Watching dampers, slowly engage dampers with the pedal or by hand by gently pulling up on connection rod.
  3. Look for early dampers.
  4. Fix early dampers by gently bending damper wire forward at the bottom of the wire.
  5. Look for late dampers.
  6. Fix late dampers by gently bending damper wire back at the bottom of the wire.
  7. Keep fixing the worst, then the next worst, etc., until dampers engage at exactly the same time, even when you engage the damper pedal very, very gently.
  8. Make the damper heads parallel to the strings by bending at the top of the wire.
  9. Damper spoon regulation. Note: This procedure is often less-necessary on pianos that are less than ~60 years old. On pianos that are newer than 60 years, at least check it to see if improvements can be made. Often, damper spoons on these pianos will already be pretty good. Tools required: Multi tool handle, Spoon bender.
  1. Keep action in the piano for this procedure.
  2. Use one of two spoon benders, depending on configuration of action.
  3. Bend spoons so damper begins to lift when hammers are 40% to 60% of the way to the string.

9. Regulate Pedals. Just a little lost motion in each.

sections.title: 8. Grand Hammer Prep and Hanging
sections.html:
1. Unwrap hammers, make sure they are grand hammers (hammers can be mislabeled). You can tell by the length of the bass hammers. Upright bass hammers are shorter than the treble, whereas grand bass hammers are longer than the treble.
  2. Using the old section ends as reference, number with a pencil and separate the hammers by section. Write the number on the bottom side (side with printed number). Number them even if the number you write down is the same as the printed one. Your handwritten number is going to be the one that counts.
  3. Pre-voice the hammers. Use the prevoicing jig. Clamp an entire section of hammers on the jig. Quick pass with a 320 grit sanding paper strip. Then with a voicing tool, stab the hammers about 5 times on each side.
  1. Create a worksheet of the string angle in each section, number of hammers in each section, previous hammer angles, and previous bore distance (distance from the felt tip of the hammer to the center of the shank hole). To determine this piano's original bore distance, use your best judgment to estimate how much to add to your bore distance measurement to account for worn/lost felt and/or reshaping that may have been done to this piano. *Bass and treble have different bore distances.
  2. Mark with a pencil the bore distance only on the section ends. Align an entire section in front of you, you can use two straight edges and small clamps to keep them straight and still. Using another straight edge draw a line across all the hammers between the section ends. Now you have bore distance on all the hammers in this section.

Optional: you can also mark the exact center of the bore distance line with a dot or hole made with an awl to use as a guide on the drill press.

  1. Bore. You can set the angles of the bore using an original section end as reference in the hammer boring jig on the drill press.
  2. Cove. If hammers are pre prepped this might not be necessary.
  1. Arc. If hammers are pre prepped this might not be necessary.
  1. Taper all hammers.
  1. Find best hammer position. Dry fit with some masking tape hammer 88 on the action. Put the action in the piano and play key 88, changing the position until you find the best one. Do the same for its section end. Repeat on bass section.
  1. Set the grand hammer hanging jig, use section ends with set position as reference. Use Quick and Thick glue.
  2. After all hammers are hung and dry, cut and sand off the excess shanks on the hammer heads.

sections.title: 9. Grand Regulation
sections.html:
Remember, grand regulation is more of a circle rather than a line. There’s a lot of going back and re-checking steps along the way.

  1. Pre-regulation
  2. Complete necessary repairs, tightening, lubrication, etc.
  3. Bedding
  4. Travel hammers (upside down stack on a clean table).
  5. Grand Hammer Alignment to strings.
  6. Whippen alignment (cushion to capstan and jack/rep lever to knuckle).
  7. Key leveling
  8. Before you start a full regulation, ensure plenty of dip, drop, and spring strength
  1. Perform the following on only white-key section ends:
  2. Set your dip according to dip block: 10mm or 0.394".
  3. Set let off according to let off gauge.
  4. Set jack position and rep lever height.

-jack position reference

  1. Set drop according to let off.
  2. Set hammer height according to 0.020"-0.030" aftertouch.
  3. Re-check rep lever and jack position.
  4. Finalizing Aftertouch: re-check aftertouch, refine aftertouch by adjusting hammer height, not by adjusting dip or let off.
  5. Set spring strength and checking.
  6. After the previous section has been completed on white section ends only, complete the following:
  7. Even out each section end with its pair (compromise, but slightly favor the higher one). Each section could have a slightly different height, but now is the time to compromise the 2.
  8. ROUGH hammer line according to section ends-quick pass!
  9. Jack position and rep lever height.
  10. Let off with jig.
  11. Drop.
  12. Spring strength.
  13. Checking.
  14. Re-check jack position and rep lever height.
  15. Refine hammer line with the help of fallboard & key slip.
  16. Dip: don't use dip block, but set your dip according to 0.020"-0.030" aftertouch. Use a gauge or punchings.

sections.title: 10. Grand Cleaning - Refurb level
sections.html:
1. Start a labeled box for small parts.
  2. Remove all cabinetry and lid.
  3. Remove action, take it outside, blow it out well with compressed air.
  4. Scotch-Brite the tuning pins, if necessary.
  5. Scotch-Brite the strings, if necessary.
  6. Use an old paint brush with the vacuum: stir up as much dust and debris with one hand and vacuum it up with the other. Do this to as much of the plate, soundboard, strings, tuning pins, and action cavity as you can reach.
  7. Vacuum under the soundboard: tops of the large support beams, above the rear leg, trapwork, etc.
  8. Soundboard Cleaning: How you do the next step will depend on what type of finish the soundboard and plate have and what condition they are in:
  9. Option 1: older pianos with dust really caked on the soundboard, clear coat on the soundboard is gone or in poor condition, sometimes with spills or stains, poor condition plate and soundboard:

    Use masking tape to mask off all gold-colored, painted plate surfaces, with the exception of the tuning pin area.

Mask off black (graphite) surfaces in the center of bridges.

Put the piano up on its side and take it outside.

Soda blast the soundboard and bridges-be thorough enough that you don't leave any streaks or stripes.

From a distance (so you don't remove gold paint), soda blast around the tuning pins and other tight, hard-to-clean areas, at your discretion.* You need to find the happy-medium where the dust and debris is removed but the paint remains intact. The distance and pressure you use will vary from piano to piano.

  1. Option 2: newer/somewhat newer pianos with dust that, once removed with a comparatively easy swipe, reveals a nicely lacquered soundboard and plate:

Use the 3 soundboard squeegees with red felt (1 large, 1 medium, 1 small) to squeegee the soundboard:

Wearing ear protection, leave the vacuum running the whole time. Insert the squeegee, wipe the soundboard, pull it out and vacuum off the dust. Repeat, repeat, repeat.

Fold lots of pieces of paper towel to make lots of fairly small squares, then use these with a sharpened hammer shank and some cleaner to detail the soundboard. Push the little paper towel squares around on the soundboard with the hammer shank (and pull them out with tweezers when each square is saturated) until you've covered all of the soundboard. Be careful to avoid getting any cleaner on the coils of the bass strings.

  1. If there are agraffes, polish them with brite-boy polish. Just polish the tops; since you're not removing the strings, don't worry about the sides.
  2. Clean the plate with TSP. Be conservative around the tuning pins to avoid allowing any cleaner to seep to the base of the tuning pins and into the pinblock underneath.
  3. Use Q-tips and a little plastic cup of cleaner to detail the plate and bridges in hard-to-reach areas. Go through 20-50 (or more!) Q-tips.

*The goal is to minimize this, as it is time-consuming. As long as you're not removing gold paint with the soda blaster, go for it in these hard-to-reach areas!

**Use very little cleaner around the tuning pins and the strings. If cleaner gets around the tuning pins, it can leak down the pin into the pinblock, and the pins would then have to be replaced.

  1. Clean damper heads.

sections.title: 11. Perimeter, Ribs, Soundboard and Bridges
sections.html:
1. Check the soundboard perimeter for any separation. If there’s separation and it’s in a spot that will allow it, just clamp it with wood glue. If not, repair just as you wood rib separation (check point 3).
  2. Check for rib separation. You can use a small metallic ruler to check and slide between the rib and soundboard if separation is suspected. There is sometimes a little separation that you can’t fit the ruler into. Check by pushing on the soundboard right by the rib shining a flashlight and see if you see movement between the soundboard and rib. Push on both sides of the rib.

You can use the ruler if it fits to stop the drill at the right depth if you want during the repair.

  1. Repair any rib separation:
  2. Slide 2 pieces of piano wire through the cracks where the separation is to be able to identify where the rib is from above the soundboard.
  3. Mark where you’ll drill in the holes for the 2 screws that will clamp the soundboard to the rib on each side of the crack. Sometimes it is on both sides of the crack, sometimes just on one side. Only need to do the side with separation.
  4. Drill in the holes for a size 8 x 1 phillips screw. Use 2 drill bits, one that’s big enough so that the screw slides through the soundboard and another that’s small enough for the screw to bite into the rib.
  5. Using a syringe and needle, inject wood glue between the soundboard and rib, try to shim the crack open a bit and then inject the glue, you can also use a plastic blade to push the glue in.
  6. Screw in the screws tight, do this by hand (it’s safer and more satisfying).
  7. Clean up under the soundboard, remove the masking tape before it’s completely dry. Let glue dry 24 hours.
  8. Once glue is dry, remove the screws, drill out the hole with #1 drill bit, plug the hole with a hammer shank and more wood glue. Let dry for a couple of hours and sand down.
  9. Restore the crown by clamping a large wooden board under the piano and a reverse clamp pushing into the center of the soundboard (on a rib, not directly on soundboard) and tighten as much as you dare. Cracking sounds are expected.
  10. Repairing soundboard cracks. You need: soundboard router, straight edge, teflon powder, nut driver for router, white masking tape, blue masking tape, N⁰ II Titebond wood glue, shims and planer. Maybe: scratcher tool, exacto knife.
  11. Restore the crown by clamping a large wooden board under the piano and a reverse clamp pushing into the center of the soundboard (on a rib, not directly on soundboard) and tighten as much as you dare. Cracking sounds are expected.
  12. Before repairing cracks make sure you already repaired any rib / perimeter separation.
  13. Make sure the metal guide (straight edge) isn’t wobbly. You can regulate this by adding punchings on the bottom sides.
  14. Secure metal guide next to the crack using white masking tape on the length of both sides and ends. You can also use double sided tape on the bottom.
  15. Apply teflon powder on the guide and where the feet of the router jig slides. Test to make sure it’s moving straight and you’re at the right distance from the crack.
  16. Router jig slides through the middle notch.
  17. Set the depth of the router bit in a spot where you can see the thickness of the soundboard, usually at the top in grands and against the edge of the plat post holes.
  18. Adjust the height as you go. Go little by little until you’re almost through the soundboard but not all the way through. Multiple passes of the same cut until you are deep enough will keep the jig from wanting to move, keep the line straight.
  19. When cracks go all the way to the edge of the piano or a bridge, rout as far as it can go. Then, with the scratcher tool scratch the rest of the crack to start it out, then with an exacto knife cut the wood into the shape of a shim. Remember to dry fit before gluing.
  20. Tape the bottom of the piano (where the cracks are) with blue or black masking tap. Also tape on the sides of the trench to make it easier to clean afterwards.
  21. Dry fit the shims and make sure everything is okay. If the shim is wobbly side to side, you need to shave off a little bit of the tip with a hand sized planer.
  22. Pour in some N⁰ II Titebond wood glue into the trench, hammer the shims in with a mallet and piece of wood to get the entire length. Push on shims to test if it is tight in the trench. For stubborn parts use a little block of wood and the grand key leveling weights box on top (to keep that side down).
  23. Remove the masking tape before it completely dries for cleanup. You’re good to start sanding down the shims after the glue is visibly dry. Once everything is dry and sanded, you can remove the clamps holding the crown tension.
  24. Repairing / Replacing Bridge cap
  25. If the original bridges only have a few, hairline small thin cracks, you can just inject super thin CA glue (blue bottle) into those cracks.
  26. To replace a cracked bridge cap first remove old bridge pins, store them in baggies separately according to area and bridge. Bridge pins are different sizes on the bass bridge (usually 2 sizes, one for monochords and one for bichords) than on the treble/tenor bridge. Use a caliper to measure them, the size difference should be noticeable by sight.
  27. Draw a mylar template of the old bridge cap. Cut out a piece bigger than the bridge cap, drill two holes into the bridge cap (one on each end, sometimes in the middle if re-capping a longer bridge) big enough so that a thin nail can slide in through all the way into the root, place the mylar on the bridge cap and punch through the mylar into the nail holes to hold it still and straight.
  28. Draw the outline of the bridge cap with a sharpie, the line should be on the outer edge of the wood. Also draw the chisel lines, where the dag edges are. Mark the pin size area separations with a dotted line and write down the new pin size on each section. With an awl, punch through the mylar into each bridge pin hole to mark their locations.
  29. Rout off old bridge cap. Sometimes you can remove bass bridges entirely from the soundboard and cut off the bridge cap from the root using the table saw.
  1. Fill in the remaining pin holes in the root with shoe pegs and wood glue, don’t fill in the nail holes. Cut off and sand excess. Leave a flat and smooth surface on the root.
  2. Cut a piece of new bridge cap material slightly bigger than the mylar template. Use the bandsaw.
  3. Place the new bridge cap material on the root and make sure it’s sitting flat and not wobbly. Now is the time to make any corrections on the root’s surface if needed.
  4. Drill the 2 nail holes into the new cap and use the nails to anchor it into place.
  5. Drill a few holes (quantity depends on the length of the bridge but for a bass bridge length it's usually 3 holes) into the the new cap with a big enough bit size so that size 8 x 1 phillips screws can slide through and drill with a small enough bit into the root so that the screws bite into the root. These will act as our clamps.
  6. Dry test with screws first to make sure it’s a tight fit.
  7. Apply N⁰ II Titebond wood glue on the root and place the new cap on top, screw in the screws by hand nice and tight. Clean any excess glue that pours out. Let dry for 24 hrs.
  8. Once dry, plug the screw holes with hammer shanks and sand.
  9. Rout out the outline of the new bridge cap using the root as a guide.
  10. Rout / plane / sand down the height of the new bridge cap according to downbearing.

Find Proper Downbearing: Targets
Depending on the location (bass / tenor / treble), stop routing when the string barely touches the bottom of the trench at the same time it touches the spacer (front rail paper punching).

  1. Bass: Zero to slight bearing (recommendation to use size .010” punching)
  2. Low tenor: Zero or slight bearing about .010” - .020”
  3. High tenor into treble: .040” - .060”
  4. High treble: .060” - .070”
  5. Once proper downbearing is achieved, place the mylar template on the new cap using the nail holes. With a pencil, mark where the bridge pin holes will go and the chisel lines. Then remove the mylar and punch into the bridge pin marks with the matching bridge pin punch. Drill into the holes using a bit according to the new bridge pin sizes and using the “bridge pin angle jig”. Double check you are drilling correct angle.
  6. With a saw or razor blade cut into the chisel lines, mark a line with pencil on the side of the bridge to determine depth and cut down until the line is reached. Then paint dag on the bridge cap.
  7. Chisel the new bridge cap. Sharpen the chisel frequently.
  1. Hammer in the new bridge pins. Afterwards, cut them down to length. Look at other bridge capping jobs for reference.

sections.title: 12. Restringing
sections.html:
1. Install plate: Find the plate cart and 3 to 4 people to help you install the plate, or use the plate hoist in the main shop. Make sure all posts have are in the plate, they’re sometimes removed when repairing the soundboard.
  2. Install perimeter screws and bolts as tight as they’ll go, then check downbearing, there shouldn’t be any negative downbearing. The desired downbearing targets probably aren’t met by older pianos with original bridges but as long as it’s not negative we’re good. If there is negative downbearing, correct it. Ask RSB trainer for guidance.

    Downbearing: Targets
  3. Bass: Zero to slight bearing (recommendation to use size .010” punching)
  4. Low tenor: Zero or slight bearing about .010” - .020”
  5. High tenor into treble: .040” - .060”
  6. High treble: .060” - .070”
  7. Install all screws and bolts, you have to do a final check once they’re all in. If they’re being stubborn try blowing out the hole and/or rubbing bee’s was on the screw/bolt.
  8. Glue the new plate felt according to before photos and using the old felts as templates.
  9. Sand the lip at the top where the strings sit. Don’t go too far.
  10. Mark the plate with blue masking tape at each point where the string size changes in the tuning pin holes area. Use teardown sheet for reference.
  11. Use foam sheets to protect the plate / pinblock
  12. Find the correct size tuning pins. Measure the old tuning pin, find it’s match and then go one size up.
  13. Lay a blanket on the piano to set all your tools and tuning pins.
  14. Wear disposable latex / nitrile gloves.
  15. Restring
  1. Before you start restringing the bass section, thread the ribbon through the strings. It's easier now than later. After restringing the bass section also thread the ribbon at the bottom and top if there’s room for it.

sections.title: 13. Keys
sections.html:
FKS stands for “Full Key Service” which includes: cleaning, rebushing and replacing keytops.

  1. Preparation:
  2. Remove keys from piano, keep keys in order and place keys on black and white pinboard to prepare for sodablasting. Mask off Sharps and keytops if either one isn’t being replaced.
  3. Soda Blast key sticks. Blow off thoroughly when finished. Blow out in between keysticks, as well as briefly inside center rail bushing holes.
  4. Remove keys from black and white pinboard. Place keysticks on table in order, group keysets by the angle of the keysticks.
  5. Number the keys. Take the Stamper, find an edge on the keysticks to consistently line up the stamper with, and number the 88 keys.
  6. Removing Key Tops - Naturals: There are 2 ways of doing this, by hand or using the router. Do whichever is more efficient at the moment. By hand you should use the heat gun and the gerber knife or box cutter knife.
  1. Removing key fronts: some fronts come off with the top when removing by hand. If it’s very thin separate plastic (like with ivory tops) it’s harder to remove by hand than to use router #2.

  1. Remove bushings (only if this is a FKS project, if only replacing keytops skip to step 6): there’s 2 options, remove with steamer or with wall paper remover. If possible use the wallpaper remover option because you can start rebushing right away after you’re done removing the old bushings, with the steamer method you have to let it dry overnight.
  2. With wallpaper remover: in a small bottle mix one part wallpaper remover and 3 parts water. Fill a 1 oz needle top bottle with the mix. Inject the product into the bushings of 10 -12 keys at a time, then go back to the first one and remove the old bushings with a tweezer. Front and balance rail. Once you reach the last soaked bushing, repeat the process on the following 10-12 keys.
  3. With steamer: steam out old bushings, insert Mortise Sizing Cauls and let dry overnight.
  4. Glue in new bushings:
  5. Measure the front and balance rail pins thickness with a caliper and write those measurements down. Find the best matching key mortice cauls. Use the key bushing felt samples in the baggie to determine which size to use.
  6. First glue one side of the balance rail using the single shoulder “ss” cauls. Use a razor blade or exacto knife to cut as you go. The glue should be an extremely thin uniform layer so that it’s strong enough to keep the bushing in place but doesn’t seep through the felt. Make sure the felt doesn’t go in deeper than it needs to.
  7. Glue the other side of the balance rail using the double shoulder “ds” cauls.
  8. Repeat the same procedure for the front rail. Main difference is that you leave felt shoulders on the front when cutting off the felt whereas on the balance rail you cut the felt flush the edge of the mortise. You can leave the keys overhanging on the edge of the table as they dry or flip them upside down.
  9. Gluing new keytops:
  10. Run a 1mm bead of pvc-e or keytop glue down the top of the cut area of the keystick. Run about a 2mm bead across the front of the keystick.
  11. Brush glue evenly across top and front of key. Not too thick of a layer, the glue layer should be somewhat translucent.
  12. Place the new keytop on. Line up the longest side of the new keytop with the keystick. If the new keytop has the tail in the center, make either side of the head line up with the edge, and make sure one side of the key tail lines up with the edge of the keystick.
  13. Glue 3-8 key this way, this gives the glue some time to get tacky and hold the key better in place before banding.
  14. Rubber band the keys. Stretch a large rubber band from the front of the keystick to the back of the keystick. This pulls the key tight against the front. Rubber band the rest of the keytop with small rubber bands. This pulls the top of the key tight against the rest of the keystick.
  15. Let keys dry overnight
  1. Key Trimming:
  2. The new keytops have excess plastic that needs to be trimmed off, the quickest way is to use multiple routers.
  3. Trim the tail of the key in Router #3
  4. Use router #4 to remove extra plastic and create a clean 90 angle cut near the back of the key head for the notches.
  1. Key filing:
  2. Start by filing the tail of the key. Make the sides of the keytop even with the keystick. File off any bits of plastic or glue the routers missed. Lightly smooth off top edge of key top tail, it will be a sharp edge from all the filing, you just need to smooth it a little to make it not as sharp. Touch up the 90 degree angle cut notch if needed.
  3. File the fronts of the key. Usually only one side needs filing. Round off the edges to match the contour of the key before it was filed.
  4. Recreate corners on the lip of the key. Be gentle on this part, it only takes a little sanding with the small block to recreate the corners.
  5. File down any seams on the front of the key lip
  6. Touch up the filed parts of the key on the polisher.
  1. Final Filing of Keys
  2. Place keys inside piano or on keybed
  3. Check clearance between sharps and naturals. Move keys up and down to see if they hit any sharps
  4. Make sure no key tops rub against other keys
  5. File the notches as needed to make two keys next to each other line up.
  6. Reshape and refile any keys as needed if they accidentally were shaped wrong from filing.
  7. Removing Sharps
  8. Place Sharp in bench vice. Line up where the sharp meets the keystick with the top of the clamp, so only the sharp is above the clamp, and only the keystick in clamped
  9. Tighten clamp
  10. Lightly tap the sharp on both sides with a small hammer. It will usually come off with a bit of tapping.
  11. If any of the keystick breaks off with the sharp, carefully remove stuck wood from sharp with a razor blade, and glue broken pieces back on keystick and clamp while drying
  12. After sharp is removed, lightly file down tops of keystick to prepare a level surface for new sharps
  13. Sizing Sharps
  14. If the replacement sharps are longer than the original sharps they need to be shortened
  15. Use Sharp Shortening Jig and Belt Sander to shorten sharps or table saw or band saw.



  16. Glueing Sharps
  17. Do dry fit of the sharp and keystick. If sharp rocks, file keystick or sharp as necessary.
  18. Hold sharp upside down
  19. Get a good thick bead of pvc-e/keytop glue inside of the sharp, it doesn’t need to be precise.
  20. Put a thin bead of glue along the bottom of the sharp. Use finger on the outside edge of the sharp to push glue back into the center of the sharp. This makes a very fine bead of glue only covering half of the bottom of the sharp.
  21. Place glued sharp on key stick. Make sure sides are centered. Make sure front of sharp is even with front of keystick.
  22. Place aside where it won’t get knocked over and let dry for a few hours.

sections.title: 14. Storing Cabinetry
sections.html:
1. The amount of shelves you will need will depend on the level of restoration you’re working with:
  2. FULL REBUILD: 3 shelves. One shelf for external cabinetry parts, the flattest piece being at the bottom and moving your way up from flattest to least flat piece. If you’re working on a grand the lid goes labeled against a wall. Put foam sheets or cardboard between EACH PIECE. Think of it as layering a cake. Another shelf will be for storing the action and one box (be it piano box or key box). Third shelf will have the keybed, trapwork / pedal board sitting on top, old bass strings and a box on the side.
  3. HYBRID REBUILD: 2 shelves. One shelf for external cabinetry parts, the flattest piece being at the bottom and moving your way up from flattest to least flat piece. If you’re working on a grand the lid goes labeled against a wall. Put foam sheets or cardboard between EACH PIECE. Think of it as layering a cake. There should still be room for a box on the side, shelves are long enough. Another shelf will be for storing the action and one box (be it piano box or key box).
  4. REFURB: 1 shelf for storing external cabinetry parts, the flattest piece being at the bottom and moving your way up from flattest to least flat piece. If you’re working on a grand the lid goes labeled against a wall. Put foam sheets or cardboard between EACH PIECE. Think of it as layering a cake.

sections.title: 15. QC Checklist
sections.html:
1. TUNING: Tuned to A440
  2. PEDALS:
  3. Squeak free (Try the pedals many times fast, medium and ask slow as possible to look for squeaks or noise). For Hailun grands, ensure sustain pedal bushing has been changed.
  4. DAMPERS: engage the sustain pedal, and while holding it down, run your fingernail along all the strings in a section then release the sustain pedal as slowly as you can. This will reveal inefficient dampers.
  5. SUSTAIN PEDAL: Make sure there is slight lost motion in the sustain pedal
  6. SOSTENUTO PEDAL: make sure the blade misses all the tabs when all the tabs are down. While holding the sustain pedal down, engage the sostenuto, then release the sustain to ensure that all the tabs catch. Oppositely, while holding the sostenuto pedal down, engage the sustain a couple times and make sure the tabs don’t get caught.
  7. SHIFT PEDAL: engage the shift pedal fully and make sure it is not going too far by listening for hammers that strike their neighbor (and look for hammers that seem too close). If any are going too far, adjust the stop screw on the treble side cheek block.
  8. Lubricate both ends of trapwork connection rods generously with Teflon powder
  9. Ensure slight lost motion in all pedals
  10. Pedal aesthetics: parallel, even in height, spaced evenly, centered in their respective slots
  11. Wiggle pedals left & right to make sure there is not any play
  12. HARDWARE
  13. Plating & buffing look good
  14. All screws tight
  15. REGULATION / ACTION:
  16. Ensure action brackets are fully against the plate and nose bolts are tight
  17. Check let off
  18. Look for jack springs amd hammer butt springs out of place
  19. Make sure after touch is not too much or too little, and is consistent
  20. Check for too little after touch by playing with a weak hand and look for bobbling hammers
  21. Check for too much after touch by playing with a strong hand and look for hammers blocking from the back checks (the proper solution to this is not to fix the back checks but to decrease dip which decreases after touch.)
  22. Check hammer alignment
  23. Check hammer spacing
  24. Make sure back checks are straight, parallel, and evenly spaced
  25. Ensure hammers check at 2/3 to ¾ of blow distance
  26. Check hammer flange screws are tight (if the first few are loose, you’ll probably have to tighten all action screws, not just the hammer flanges)
  27. Ensure bridle wires are parallel and evenly spaced, with slight slack in bridle strap
  28. (Grands) Ensure correct spring strength, jack position, rep lever height.
  29. KEYS:
  30. Ensure keys are level, square, and evenly spaced
  31. Engage the sustain pedal, play every note and release as slowly as possible to identify tight bushings that need to be eased
  32. Play every note, very gently, just a fraction of an inch, listening for glue residue in the bushings, listening for smacks or squeaks in the bushings
  33. Push on the key slip to try to make keys stick. If you can push the key slip into the front of the keys, either shim the key slip or turn the key slip capstans off the piano so that it is impossible to push the key slip into the keys. (be sure you don’t shim too far by engaging the shift pedal to ensure that the action can still return to its home position.
  34. Lift each key slightly to ensure up stop rail isn’t touching
  35. BENCH: Tightened & Squeak Free

  36. CABINETRY:
  37. Check all moving cabinetry parts move freely and squeak free.
  38. No missing rubber bumpers.
  39. CLEANING: Dust & fingerprint free
  40. FINISH: Any scratches or refinishing blemishes to report to the refinishing shop?
  41. FINAL CHECK: Play it for at least 5 minutes, listening for squeaks, rattles, knocks or clicks, buzzes or touch issues.
  42. Make sure “After Photos” have been taken either by the shop photographer or by you if they’re not available. Follow the photography handbook guidelines for these photos.
  43. Let the admin know that the QC is finished so that they can make an after video.
